evals.prompt:
I'm starting a CX transformation project for a retail bank. Here's the context I have:

**Revenue & Growth:** Revenue grew 4% last year, mostly from rate increases not volume. New customer acquisition is flat. Home loan book is growing but margins are compressed.

**Customer Metrics:** NPS is 23, down from 34 three years ago. CSAT for branch is 72%, for digital is 58%. Complaint volumes up 18% YoY. Top complaint themes: billing errors, delayed responses, digital glitches.

**Operations:** Cost-to-serve has increased 12% in 2 years. First contact resolution is 61%. Average handle time in the contact centre is up. Digital self-service completion rate is 34%.

**Market:** Market share is holding at 8% but premium positioning is eroding. Two neobanks have launched in the last 18 months with significantly better digital ratings.

**Org:** CX team was restructured 6 months ago. There are 3 competing CX programs currently active with no shared governance. Exec sponsor for CX changed twice in 18 months.

I need this analysis to inform a board paper on why we need a major CX transformation investment.
evals.expected_output: A five-domain diagnostic with honest health ratings supported by the provided data. Should identify 3-5 specific tensions between the data points. Strategic So What should connect directly to a board-level investment case.
expectations: Each of the five domains is assessed with a health rating (Strong/Adequate/At Risk/Critical) supported by specific data points from the input
Customer Metrics domain is rated At Risk or Critical given the 11-point NPS decline and 18% complaint volume increase
Organizational Capability domain flags the exec sponsor instability and competing programs as specific evidence
At least 3 tensions are identified — and they must name specific conflicting data points from the input (not generic tensions)
The tension between flat acquisition and NPS decline is named (new customers entering a worsening experience)
The Strategic So What connects to a board investment case — mentions the specific risk of inaction using the data provided
Data gaps are flagged — the input doesn't include CLV, churn rate, or employee data, and the output should note these
No claim is made without traceable evidence from the input — no hallucinated metrics

evals.prompt:
I need a business context analysis for an aged care provider before we start their patient/resident experience redesign. Here's what I know:

- Revenue is government-funded (NDIS + residential aged care subsidies) with some private pay. Subsidy rates were cut 3% last year following a government review.
- Family satisfaction surveys show scores of 64% 'satisfied or very satisfied' — below the sector benchmark of 71%.
- Staff turnover is 38% per year in care roles. Agency staff make up 22% of the workforce on any given day.
- Royal Commission into Aged Care Quality and Safety reported 2 years ago — significant regulatory overhang and compliance requirements.
- CEO is 8 months into the role, came from acute health. Board wants visible CX improvement within 12 months.
- No dedicated CX function. Quality and Compliance team owns most resident-facing quality metrics.

This analysis will inform our discovery scope — what to focus on and what to tell the new CEO about what's actually going on.
evals.expected_output: Five-domain diagnostic appropriate for aged care context, with tensions that reflect the specific dynamics of government-funded care (regulatory pressure, workforce instability, quality compliance vs. experience design). So What should address the 12-month board expectation.
expectations: Revenue domain addresses the government subsidy model and the implications of the 3% cut — not treated like a commercial business
Operational Health domain prominently flags the 38% turnover and 22% agency workforce as a CX delivery risk (not just an HR issue)
Organizational Capability domain notes the absence of a CX function and the Quality/Compliance ownership of resident metrics as a structural gap
A tension is identified between the 12-month board expectation for visible improvement and the structural depth of the workforce problem
The Royal Commission regulatory context is reflected in the analysis
Strategic So What gives the new CEO a clear framing for what the data shows and what the discovery scope should prioritise
The output distinguishes between what is evidenced by the data provided and what is inferred from context

evals.prompt:
Quick business context read for a pitch I'm writing. The client is a large Australian telco. I don't have their internal data but I know:
- They're the #2 player by market share (roughly 25%)
- Their NPS has historically lagged the market leader by 8-12 points
- They've had two major network outages in the last year that got national media coverage
- They're investing heavily in 5G rollout
- Their customer service has been restructured twice in 3 years
- Industry churn benchmark is around 14% per year

I'm pitching for a CX transformation engagement. I need this analysis to feel credible and specific — using what I know to build a picture of where the pressure is.
evals.expected_output: A situation analysis that works from limited public/contextual information, clearly flagging what's inferred vs evidenced. Should build a credible CX opportunity framing for a pitch context.
expectations: The output clearly labels claims as [EVIDENCED], [INFERRED], or [ASSUMED] (or equivalent tagging) — given the limited inputs
The network outage events are treated as a customer trust and brand perception issue in the Market Position domain
The repeated restructuring of customer service is flagged as an Organizational Capability risk — not normalised
A tension is identified between the 5G investment (future growth) and current NPS gap (present experience deficit)
Data gaps register is comprehensive — notes missing NPS score, churn rate, cost-to-serve data that would strengthen the analysis
The Strategic So What is written for a pitch context — addresses why CX transformation is the right opportunity now for this client
The output does not fabricate specific metrics — all numbers either come from the input or are explicitly labelled as estimated/benchmarked
